current_status_summary: Pivoting to a mathematically rigorous and syntactically valid formalization of two-way communication complexity. Implementing the correct Cut-and-Paste (Splicing) lemma for Turing Machine crossing sequences to bound two-way interactive algorithms without type mismatches.
acknowledged_critic_fallacies: Acknowledged the Lean 4 Syntax and Logic Failure. The previous `cut_paste_property` incorrectly asserted `Out(x1,y1) = Out(x2,y2)` instead of the required mixed-input property `Out(x1,y2) = Out(x1,y1)`. Acknowledged the type mismatches caused by passing tuple pairs instead of individual elements to the hypothesis. These errors broke the proof and failed to capture two-way TM dynamics.
alpha_directive: Define the correct two-way Cut-and-Paste property: `def CutAndPaste := \forall x1 y1 x2 y2, CS (x1, y1) = CS (x2, y2) \rightarrow Out (x1, y2) = Out (x1, y1)`. Write a rigorous Lean 4 proof for `fooling_set_implies_CS_injective_on_S`. Fix the type mismatches by passing individual arguments (e.g., `h_cut p1.1 p1.2 p2.1 p2.2 hCS`) instead of tuples. Add a hypothesis `h_diag : \forall p1 p2 \in S, p1.1 = p2.1 \rightarrow p1 = p2` to handle the diagonal uniqueness, and close the proof without `sorry`.
beta_directive: Apply the corrected Cut-and-Paste property to the FRSB landscape. Define `FRSB_Target k x y := (x = y)`. Prove `FRSB_CutAndPaste_Crossing_Sequence_Bound` by assuming `CS(p.1, p.2) = CS(q.1, q.2)` for distinct diagonal elements `p` and `q`. Use the corrected `cut_paste_property` to deduce `Out(p.1, q.2) = Out(p.1, p.2)`. Show that correctness forces this to evaluate to `false = true`, yielding a contradiction. Conclude that `CS` is injective on the diagonal.
critic_target: Verify that the Lean 4 code compiles perfectly with no type mismatches. Ensure the `CutAndPaste` property correctly abstracts two-way TM boundary crossings (where the output on the spliced input `(x1, y2)` matches the original) and strictly enforces the `|Q|^c \geq 2^k` bound for two-way TMs without violating Algorithmic Agnosticism.
